By default, the file will be created in the directory defined by the variable maxima_tempdir, unless file_name contains the character “/”, in which case it will be assumed to contain the complete path where the file should be created. The value of maxima_tempdir can be changed to save the file in a different directory. When the option gnuplot_pdf_term_command is also given, it will be used to set up Gnuplot’s PDF terminal; otherwise, Gnuplot’s pdfcairo terminal will be used with solid colored lines of width 3, plot size of 17.2 cm by 12.9 cm and font of 18 points.

pdf_gamma.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a $\Gamma\left(a,b\right)$ random variable, with $a,b>0$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_general_finite_discrete.summary: Returns the value at x of the probability function of a general finite discrete random variable, with vector probabilities $v$, such that $Pr(X=i) = v_i$. Vector $v$ can be a list of nonnegative expressions whose components will be normalized to get a vector of probabilities. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_geometric.summary: Returns the value at x of the probability function of a ${\it Geometric}(p)$ random variable, with $0 < p \leq 1$

pdf_gumbel.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a ${\it Gumbel}(a,b)$ random variable, with $b>0$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_hypergeometric.summary: Returns the value at x of the probability function of a ${\it Hypergeometric}(n1,n2,n)$ random variable, with $n_1$, $n_2$ and $n$ non negative integers and $n\leq n_1+n_2$. Being $n_1$ the number of objects of class A, $n_2$ the number of objects of class B, and $n$ the size of the sample without replacement, this function returns the probability of event "exactly x objects are of class A".

pdf_laplace.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a ${\it Laplace}(a,b)$ random variable, with $b>0$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_logistic.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a ${\it Logistic}(a,b)$ random variable , with $b>0$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_lognormal.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a ${\it Lognormal}(m,s)$ random variable, with $s>0$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_negative_binomial.summary: Returns the value at x of the probability function of a ${\it NegativeBinomial}(n,p)$ random variable, with $0 < p \leq 1$ and $n$ a positive number. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_noncentral_chi2.summary: Returns the value at $x$ of the density function of a noncentral $\chi^2$ random variable m4_noncentral_chi2(n,ncp) , with $n>0$ and noncentrality parameter $ncp \ge 0.$ To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_noncentral_student_t.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a noncentral Student random variable ${\it nc\_t}(n, ncp)$ , with $n>0$ degrees of freedom and noncentrality parameter $ncp$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_normal.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a ${\it Normal}(m, s)$ random variable, with $s>0$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_pareto.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a ${\it Pareto}(a,b)$ random variable, with $a,b>0$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_poisson.summary: Returns the value at x of the probability function of a ${\it Poisson}(m)$ random variable, with $m>0$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_rank_sum.summary: Probability density function of the exact distribution of the rank sum statistic. Argument x is a real number and n and m are both positive integers.

pdf_rayleigh.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a ${\it Rayleigh}(b)$ random variable, with $b>0$.

pdf_signed_rank.summary: Probability density function of the exact distribution of the signed rank statistic. Argument x is a real number and n a positive integer.

pdf_student_t.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a Student random variable $t(n)$ , with $n>0$ degrees of freedom. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

pdf_weibull.summary: Returns the value at x of the density function of a ${\it Weibull}(a,b)$ random variable, with $a,b>0$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

perm_cycles.summary: Returns permutation p as a product of cycles. The cycles are written in a canonical form, in which the lowest index in the cycle is placed in the first position.

perm_decomp.summary: Returns the minimum set of adjacent transpositions whose product equals the given permutation p.

perm_inverse.summary: Returns the inverse of a permutation of p, namely, a permutation q such that the products pq and qp are equal to the identity permutation: [1, 2, 3, ..., n], where n is the length of p.

perm_length.summary: Determines the minimum number of adjacent transpositions necessary to write permutation p as a product of adjacent transpositions. An adjacent transposition is a cycle with only two numbers, which are consecutive integers.

perm_lex_next.summary: Returns the permutation that comes after the given permutation p, in the sequence of permutations in lexicographic order.

perm_lex_rank.summary: Finds the position of permutation p, an integer from 1 to the degree n of the permutation, in the sequence of permutations in lexicographic order.

perm_lex_unrank.summary: Returns the n-degree permutation at position i (from 1 to n!) in the lexicographic ordering of permutations.

perm_next.summary: Returns the permutation that comes after the given permutation p, in the sequence of permutations in Trotter-Johnson order.

perm_parity.summary: Finds the parity of permutation p: 0 if the minimum number of adjacent transpositions necessary to write permutation p as a product of adjacent transpositions is even, or 1 if that number is odd.

perm_rank.summary: Finds the position of permutation p, an integer from 1 to the degree n of the permutation, in the sequence of permutations in Trotter-Johnson order.

perm_undecomp.summary: Converts the list of cycles cl of degree n into an n degree permutation, equal to their product.

perm_unrank.summary: Returns the n-degree permutation at position i (from 1 to n!) in the Trotter-Johnson ordering of permutations.

permanent.summary: Computes the permanent of the matrix M by the Johnson-Gentleman tree minor algorithm.  A permanent is like a determinant but with no sign changes. permanent returns the result in CRE form.

permp.summary: Returns true if p is a valid permutation namely, a list of length n, whose elements are all the positive integers from 1 to n, without repetitions. Otherwise, it returns false.

perms.summary: perms(n) returns a list of all n-degree permutations in the so-called Trotter-Johnson order.

perms_lex.summary: perms_lex(n) returns a list of all n-degree permutations in the so-called lexicographic order.

permult.summary: Returns the product of two or more permutations p_1, ..., p_m.

permut.summary: returns the list of permutations of the list L.

permutation.summary: Returns the number of permutations of r objects selected from a set of n objects.

permutations.summary: Returns a set of all distinct permutations of the members of the list or set a. Each permutation is a list, not a set.

permute.summary: Applies the permutation p to the elements of the list (or set) l.

petersen_graph.summary: Returns the petersen graph P_{n,d}. The default values for n and d are n=5 and d=2.

petrov.summary: Computes the Petrov classification of the metric characterized by psi[0]...psi[4].

pickapart.summary: Assigns intermediate expression labels to subexpressions of expr at depth n, an integer.  Subexpressions at greater or lesser depths are not assigned labels.  pickapart returns an expression in terms of intermediate expressions equivalent to the original expression expr.

picture_equalp.summary: Returns true in case of equal pictures, and false otherwise.

picturep.summary: Returns true if the argument is a well formed image, and false otherwise.

piece.summary: Holds the last expression selected when using the part functions.

piechart.summary: Similar to barsplot, but plots sectors instead of rectangles.

piechart_description.summary: Function piechart_description creates a graphic object suitable for creating complex scenes, together with other graphic objects.

pivot_count_sx.summary: After linear_program returns, pivot_count_sx is the number of pivots in last computation.

pivot_max_sx.summary: pivot_max_sx is the maximum number of pivots allowed by linear_program.

planar_embedding.summary: Returns the list of facial walks in a planar embedding of gr and false if gr is not a planar graph.

playback.summary: Displays input, output, and intermediate expressions, without recomputing them. playback only displays the expressions bound to labels; any other output (such as text printed by print or describe, or error messages) is not displayed.  See also labels.

plog.summary: Represents the principal branch of the complex-valued natural logarithm with -%pi < carg(x) <= +%pi .

plot2d.summary: There are 5 types of plots that can be plotted by plot2d:

plot3d.summary: Displays a plot of one or more surfaces defined as functions of two variables or in parametric form.

plot_format.summary: Default value: gnuplot, in Windows systems, or gnuplot_pipes in other systems.

plot_options.summary: This variable is being kept for compatibility with older versions of Wxmaxima, but its use is deprecated. To set global plotting options, see their current values or remove options, use set_plot_option, get_plot_option remove_plot_option, and reset_005fplot_005foptions.

plotdf.summary: The function plotdf creates a two-dimensional plot of the direction field (also called slope field) for a first-order Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) or a system of two autonomous first-order ODE’s.

ploteq.summary: Plots equipotential curves for exp, which should be an expression depending on two variables. The curves are obtained by integrating the differential equation that define the orthogonal trajectories to the solutions of the autonomous system obtained from the gradient of the expression given. The plot can also show the integral curves for that gradient system (option fieldlines).

plsquares.summary: Multivariable polynomial adjustment of a data table by the "least squares" method. Mat is a matrix containing the data, VarList is a list of variable names (one for each Mat column, but use "-" instead of varnames to ignore Mat columns), depvars is the name of a dependent variable or a list with one or more names of dependent variables (which names should be in VarList), maxexpon is the optional maximum exponent for each independent variable (1 by default), and maxdegree is the optional maximum polynomial degree (maxexpon by default); note that the sum of exponents of each term must be equal or smaller than maxdegree, and if maxdgree = 0 then no limit is applied.

png_file.summary: Saves the plot into a PNG graphics file with name equal to file_name, rather than showing it in the screen. By default, the file will be created in the directory defined by the variable maxima_tempdir, unless file_name contains the character “/”, in which case it will be assumed to contain the complete path where the file should be created. The value of maxima_tempdir can be changed to save the file in a different directory. When the option gnuplot_png_term_command is also given, it will be used to set up Gnuplot’s PNG terminal; otherwise, Gnuplot’s pngcairo terminal will be used, with a font of size 12.

point_type.summary: In gnuplot, each set of points to be plotted with the style “points” or “linespoints” will be represented with objects taken from this list, in sequential order.  If there are more sets of points than objects in this list, they will be repeated sequentially. The possible objects that can be used are: bullet, circle, plus, times, asterisk, box, square, triangle, delta, wedge, nabla, diamond, lozenge.

poisdiff.summary: Differentiates a with respect to b. b must occur only in the trig arguments or only in the coefficients.

poisexpt.summary: Functionally identical to intopois (a^b). b must be a positive integer.

poisint.summary: Integrates in a similarly restricted sense (to poisdiff).  Non-periodic terms in b are dropped if b is in the trig arguments.

poismap.summary: will map the functions sinfn on the sine terms and cosfn on the cosine terms of the Poisson series given.  sinfn and cosfn are functions of two arguments which are a coefficient and a trigonometric part of a term in series respectively.

poisplus.summary: Is functionally identical to intopois (a + b).

poissimp.summary: Converts a into a Poisson series for a in general representation.

poisson.summary: The symbol /P/ follows the line label of Poisson series expressions.

poissubst.summary: Substitutes a for b in c.  c is a Poisson series.

poistrim.summary: is a reserved function name which (if the user has defined it) gets applied during Poisson multiplication.  It is a predicate function of 6 arguments which are the coefficients of the u, v, ..., z in a term.  Terms for which poistrim is true (for the coefficients of that term) are eliminated during multiplication.

polar.summary: Draws 2D functions defined in polar coordinates.

polar_to_xy.summary: It can be given as value for the transform_xy option of plot3d.  Its effect will be to interpret the two independent variables in plot3d as the distance from the z axis and the azimuthal angle (polar coordinates), and transform them into x and y coordinates.

polarform.summary: Returns an expression r %e^(%i theta) equivalent to expr, such that r and theta are purely real.

polartorect.summary: Translates complex values of the form $r e^{i t}$ to the form $a + b i$, where $r$ is the magnitude and $t$ is the phase. $r$ and $t$ are 1-dimensional arrays of the same size. The array size need not be a power of 2.

poly_add.summary: Adds two polynomials poly1 and poly2.

poly_buchberger.summary: poly_buchberger performs the Buchberger algorithm on a list of polynomials and returns the resulting Groebner basis.

poly_buchberger_criterion.summary: Returns true if polylist is a Groebner basis with respect to the current term order, by using the Buchberger criterion: for every two polynomials $h1$ and $h2$ in polylist the S-polynomial $S(h1,h2)$ reduces to 0 $modulo$ polylist.

poly_colon_ideal.summary: Returns the reduced Groebner basis of the colon ideal

poly_content.summary: poly_content extracts the GCD of its coefficients

poly_depends_p.summary: poly_depends tests whether a polynomial depends on a variable var.

poly_elimination_ideal.summary: poly_elimination_ideal returns the grobner basis of the $number$-th elimination ideal of an ideal specified as a list of generating polynomials (not necessarily Groebner basis).

poly_exact_divide.summary: Divide a polynomial poly1 by another polynomial poly2. Assumes that exact division with no remainder is possible. Returns the quotient.

poly_expand.summary: This function parses polynomials to internal form and back. It is equivalent to expand(poly) if poly parses correctly to a polynomial. If the representation is not compatible with a polynomial in variables varlist, the result is an error. It can be used to test whether an expression correctly parses to the internal representation. The following examples illustrate that indexed and transcendental function variables are allowed.

poly_expt.summary: exponentitates poly by a positive integer number. If number is not a positive integer number an error will be raised.

poly_gcd.summary: Returns the greatest common divisor of poly1 and poly2.

poly_grobner.summary: Returns a Groebner basis of the ideal span by the polynomials polylist. Affected by the global flags.

poly_grobner_equal.summary: poly_grobner_equal tests whether two Groebner Bases generate the same ideal. Returns true if two lists of polynomials polylist1 and polylist2, assumed to be Groebner Bases, generate the same ideal, and false otherwise. This is equivalent to checking that every polynomial of the first basis reduces to 0 modulo the second basis and vice versa. Note that in the example below the first list is not a Groebner basis, and thus the result is false.

poly_grobner_member.summary: Returns true if a polynomial poly belongs to the ideal generated by the polynomial list polylist, which is assumed to be a Groebner basis. Returns false otherwise.

poly_grobner_subsetp.summary: poly_grobner_subsetp tests whether an ideal generated by polylist1 is contained in the ideal generated by polylist2. For this test to always succeed, polylist2 must be a Groebner basis.

poly_ideal_intersection.summary: poly_ideal_intersection returns the intersection of two ideals.

poly_ideal_polysaturation.summary: polylistlist is a list of n list of polynomials [polylist1,...,polylistn]. Returns the reduced Groebner basis of the saturation of the ideal

poly_ideal_polysaturation1.summary: polylist2 is a list of n polynomials [poly1,...,polyn]. Returns the reduced Groebner basis of the ideal

poly_ideal_saturation.summary: Returns the reduced Groebner basis of the saturation of the ideal

poly_ideal_saturation1.summary: Returns the reduced Groebner basis of the saturation of the ideal

poly_lcm.summary: Returns the lowest common multiple of poly1 and poly2.

poly_minimization.summary: Returns a sublist of the polynomial list polylist spanning the same monomial ideal as polylist but minimal, i.e. no leading monomial of a polynomial in the sublist divides the leading monomial of another polynomial.

poly_multiply.summary: Returns the product of polynomials poly1 and poly2.

poly_normal_form.summary: poly_normal_form finds the normal form of a polynomial poly with respect to a set of polynomials polylist.

poly_normalize.summary: Returns the polynomial poly divided by the leading coefficient. It assumes that the division is possible, which may not always be the case in rings which are not fields.

poly_normalize_list.summary: poly_normalize_list applies poly_normalize to each polynomial in the list. That means it divides every polynomial in a list polylist by its leading coefficient.

poly_polysaturation_extension.summary: <!-- category: Polynomials --> <!-- keywords: poly_primary_elimination_order --> <!-- signatures: poly_primary_elimination_order -->

poly_primitive_part.summary: Returns the polynomial poly divided by the GCD of its coefficients.

poly_pseudo_divide.summary: Pseudo-divide a polynomial poly by the list of $n$ polynomials polylist. Return multiple values. The first value is a list of quotients $a$. The second value is the remainder $r$. The third argument is a scalar coefficient $c$, such that $cpoly$ can be divided by polylist* within the ring of coefficients, which is not necessarily a field. Finally, the fourth value is an integer count of the number of reductions performed. The resulting objects satisfy the equation:

poly_reduced_grobner.summary: Returns a reduced Groebner basis of the ideal span by the polynomials polylist. Affected by the global flags.

poly_reduction.summary: poly_reduction reduces a list of polynomials polylist, so that each polynomial is fully reduced with respect to the other polynomials.

poly_s_polynomial.summary: Returns the syzygy polynomial (S-polynomial) of two polynomials poly1 and poly2.

poly_saturation_extension.summary: poly_saturation_extension implements the famous Rabinowitz trick.

poly_subtract.summary: Subtracts a polynomial poly2 from poly1.

polydecomp.summary: Decomposes the polynomial p in the variable x into the functional composition of polynomials in x. polydecomp returns a list [p_1, ..., p_n] such that

polymod.summary: Converts the polynomial p to a modular representation with respect to the current modulus which is the value of the variable modulus.

polynome2ele.summary: gives the list l = [n, e_1, ..., e_n] where n is the degree of the polynomial P in the variable x and e_i is the i-the elementary symmetric function of the roots of P.

polynomialp.summary: Return true if p is a polynomial in the variables in the list L.  The predicate coeffp must evaluate to true for each coefficient, and the predicate exponp must evaluate to true for all exponents of the variables in L.  If you want to use a non-default value for exponp, you must supply coeffp with a value even if you want to use the default for coeffp.

polytocompanion.summary: If p is a polynomial in x, return the companion matrix of p. For a monic polynomial p of degree n, we have p = (-1)^n charpoly (polytocompanion (p, x)).

pop.summary: pop removes and returns the first element from the list list. The argument list must be a mapatom that is bound to a nonempty list. If the argument list is not bound to a nonempty list, Maxima signals an error. For examples, see push.

posfun.summary: declare (f, posfun) declares f to be a positive function. is (f(x) > 0) yields true.

postfix.summary: postfix operators like the prefix variety denote functions of a single argument, but in this case the argument immediately precedes an occurrence of the operator in the input string, e.g. 3!.  The postfix("x") function is a syntax extension function to declare x to be a postfix operator.

potential.summary: The calculation makes use of the global variable potentialzeroloc[0] which must be nonlist or of the form

power_mod.summary: Uses a modular algorithm to compute a^n mod m where a and n are integers and m is a positive integer. If n is negative, inv_mod is used to find the modular inverse.

powerseries.summary: Returns the general form of the power series expansion for expr in the variable x about the point a (which may be inf for infinity):

powerset.summary: Returns the set of all subsets of a, or a subset of that set.

pred.summary: As an argument in a call to ev (expr), pred causes predicates (expressions which evaluate to true or false) to be evaluated.  See ev.

prefix.summary: A prefix operator is one which signifies a function of one argument, which argument immediately follows an occurrence of the operator. prefix("x") is a syntax extension function to declare x to be a prefix operator.

prev_prime.summary: Returns the greatest prime smaller than n.

primelmt.summary: Computes a prime element for the extension of $K[a]$ by a root b of a polynomial $f_b(b)$ whose coefficients may depend on a. One assumes that f_b is square free. The function returns an irreducible polynomial, a root of which generates $K[a, b]$, and the expression of this primitive element in terms of a and b.

primep.summary: Primality test.  If primep (n) returns false, n is a composite number and if it returns true, n is a prime number with very high probability.

primes.summary: Returns the list of all primes from start to end.

principal_components.summary: Calculates the principal components of a multivariate sample. Principal components are used in multivariate statistical analysis to reduce the dimensionality of the sample.

print.summary: Evaluates and displays expr_1, ..., expr_n one after another, from left to right, starting at the left edge of the console display.

print_graph.summary: Prints some information about the graph gr.

printf.summary: Produces formatted output by outputting the characters of control-string string and observing that a tilde introduces a directive. The character after the tilde, possibly preceded by prefix parameters and modifiers, specifies what kind of formatting is desired. Most directives use one or more elements of the arguments expr_1, ..., expr_n to create their output.

printfile.summary: Prints the file named by path to the console.  path may be a string or a symbol; if it is a symbol, it is converted to a string.

printpois.summary: Prints a Poisson series in a readable format.  In common with outofpois, it will convert a into a Poisson encoding first, if necessary.

printprops.summary: Displays the property with the indicator i associated with the atom a.  a may also be a list of atoms or the atom all in which case all of the atoms with the given property will be used.  For example, printprops ([f, g], atvalue).  printprops is for properties that cannot otherwise be displayed, i.e.  for atvalue, atomgrad, gradef, and matchdeclare.

prodrac.summary: L is a list containing the elementary symmetric functions on a set A. prodrac returns the polynomial whose roots are the k by k products of the elements of A.

product.summary: Represents a product of the values of expr as the index i varies from i_0 to i_1. The noun form 'product is displayed as an uppercase letter pi.

program.summary: Defines the program used for positioning vertices of the graph. Can be one of the graphviz programs (dot, neato, twopi, circ, fdp), circular, spring_embedding or planar_embedding. planar_embedding is only available for 2-connected planar graphs. When program=spring_embedding, a set of vertices with fixed position can be specified with the fixed_vertices option.

properties.summary: Returns a list of the names of all the properties associated with the atom a.

propvars.summary: Returns a list of those atoms on the props list which have the property indicated by prop.  Thus propvars (atvalue) returns a list of atoms which have atvalues.

ps_file.summary: Saves the plot into a Postscript file with name equal to file_name, rather than showing it in the screen.  By default, the file will be created in the directory defined by the variable maxima_tempdir, unless file_name contains the character “/”, in which case it will be assumed to contain the complete path where the file should be created. The value of maxima_tempdir can be changed to save the file in a different directory. When the option gnuplot_ps_term_command is also given, it will be used to set up Gnuplot’s Postscript terminal; otherwise, Gnuplot’s postscript terminal will be used with the EPS option, solid colored lines of width 2, plot size of 16.4 cm by 12.3 cm and font of 24 points.

psi.summary: psi is the polygamma function ([https://dlmf.nist.gov/5.2E2DLMF 5.2E2](), [https://dlmf.nist.gov/5.15DLMF 5.15](), [https://personal.math.ubc.ca/~cbm/aands/page_258.htmA&S eqn 6.3.1]() and [https://personal.math.ubc.ca/~cbm/aands/page_260.htmA&S eqn 6.4.1]()) defined by

pslq_integer_relation.summary: Implements the PSLQ algorithm [1] to find integer relations between bigfloat numbers.

pslq_status.summary: Indicates success or failure for an integer relation search by pslq_integer_relation.

psubst.summary: psubst(a, b, expr) is similar to subst.  See subst.

ptriangularize.summary: If M is a matrix with each entry a polynomial in v, return a matrix M2 such that

pui.summary: decomposes the symmetric polynomial sym, in the variables in the list lvar, in terms of the power functions in the list L. If the first element of L is given, it will be the size of the alphabet, otherwise the size will be the degree of the polynomial sym.  If values are missing in the list L, formal values of the type p1, p2 , etc. will be added. The polynomial sym may be given in three different forms: contracted (elem should then be 1, its default value), partitioned (elem should be 3), or extended (i.e. the entire polynomial, and elem should then be 2). The function pui is used in the same way.

pui2comp.summary: renders the list of the first n complete functions (with the length first) in terms of the power functions given in the list lpui. If the list lpui is empty, the cardinal is n, otherwise it is its first element (as in comp2ele and comp2pui).

pui2ele.summary: effects the passage from power functions to the elementary symmetric functions. If the flag pui2ele is girard, it will return the list of elementary symmetric functions from 1 to n, and if the flag is close, it will return the n-th elementary symmetric function.

pui2polynome.summary: calculates the polynomial in x whose power functions of the roots are given in the list lpui.

pui_direct.summary: Let f be a polynomial in n blocks of variables lvar_1, ..., lvar_n.  Let c_i be the number of variables in lvar_i, and SC be the product of n symmetric groups of degree c_1, ..., c_n. This group acts naturally on f. The list orbite is the orbit, denoted SC(f), of the function f under the action of SC. (This list may be obtained by the function multi_orbit.)  The di are integers s.t. $c_1 <= d_1, c_2 <= d_2, ..., c_n <= d_n$.

puireduc.summary: lpui is a list whose first element is an integer m. puireduc gives the first n power functions in terms of the first m.

push.summary: push prepends the item item to the list list and returns a copy of the new list. The second argument list must be a mapatom that is bound to a list. The first argument item can be any Maxima symbol or expression. If the argument list is not bound to a list, Maxima signals an error.

put.summary: Assigns value to the property (specified by indicator) of atom.  indicator may be the name of any property, not just a system-defined property.

pv.summary: We can calculate the present value of a Future one given a certain interest rate. rate is the interest rate, FV is the future value and num is the number of periods.

pwilt.summary: Computes the inverse Laplace transform of expr with respect to s and parameter t. Unlike ilt, pwilt is able to return piece-wise and periodic functions and can also handle some cases with polynomials of degree greater than 3 in the denominator.

pytranslate.summary: Translates the expression expr to equivalent python3 statements. Output is printed in the stdout.

qdisplay.summary: Represents the state q of a system of n qubits as a linear combination of the computational states with n binary digits.  It returns an expression including strings and symbols.

qmatrix.summary: This variable is a predefined hash array of two by two matrices with the standard matrices: identity, Pauli matrices, Hadamard matrix and the phase matrix. The six possible indices are I, X, Y, Z, H, S. qmatrix[I] is the identity matrix, qmatrix[X] the Pauli x matrix, qmatrix[Y] the Pauli y matrix, qmatrix[Z] the Pauli z matrix, qmatrix[H] the Hadamard matrix and qmatrix[S] the phase matrix.

qmeasure.summary: Measures the value of one or more qubits in a system of n qubits with state q. The m positive integers i1, ..., im are the positions of the qubits to be measured It requires 1 or more arguments. The first argument must be the state q. If the only argument given is q, all the n qubits will be measured.

qput.summary: Assigns value to the property (specified by indicator) of atom.  This is the same as put, except that the arguments are quoted.

qrange.summary: Returns the interquartile range, defined as the difference between the third and first quartiles: quantile(x, 3/4) - quantile(x, 1/4)

qswap.summary: Interchanges the states of qubits i and j in the state q of a system of several qubits.  It modifies the list q and returns its modified value.

qty.summary: Returns the nondimensional part of a dimensional quantity x, or returns x if x is nondimensional. x may be a literal dimensional expression $a ` b$, a symbol with declared quantity, or an expression containing either or both of those.

quad_control.summary: Control error handling for quadpack.  The parameter should be one of the following symbols:

quad_qag.signature: quad_qag(f(x), x, a, b, key, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qag.summary: Integration of a general function over a finite interval.  quad_qag implements a simple globally adaptive integrator using the strategy of Aind (Piessens, 1973).  The caller may choose among 6 pairs of Gauss-Kronrod quadrature formulae for the rule evaluation component.  The high-degree rules are suitable for strongly oscillating integrands.
signatures: quad_qag(f(x), x, a, b, key, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qag(f, x, a, b, key, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])

quad_qagi.signature: quad_qagi(f(x), x, a, b, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qagi.summary: Integration of a general function over an infinite or semi-infinite interval. The interval is mapped onto a finite interval and then the same strategy as in quad_qags is applied.
signatures: quad_qagi(f(x), x, a, b, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qagi(f, x, a, b, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])

quad_qagp.signature: quad_qagp(f(x), x, a, b, points, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qagp.summary: Integration of a general function over a finite interval. quad_qagp implements globally adaptive interval subdivision with extrapolation (de Doncker, 1978) by the Epsilon algorithm (Wynn, 1956).
signatures: quad_qagp(f(x), x, a, b, points, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qagp(f, x, a, b, points, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])

quad_qags.signature: quad_qags(f(x), x, a, b, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qags.summary: Integration of a general function over a finite interval. quad_qags implements globally adaptive interval subdivision with extrapolation (de Doncker, 1978) by the Epsilon algorithm (Wynn, 1956).
signatures: quad_qags(f(x), x, a, b, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qags(f, x, a, b, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])

quad_qawc.signature: quad_qawc(f(x), x, c, a, b, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qawc.summary: Computes the Cauchy principal value of $f(x)/(x - c)$ over a finite interval.  The strategy is globally adaptive, and modified Clenshaw-Curtis integration is used on the subranges which contain the point $x = c$.
signatures: quad_qawc(f(x), x, c, a, b, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qawc(f, x, c, a, b, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])

quad_qawf.signature: quad_qawf(f(x), x, a, omega, trig, [epsabs, limit, maxp1, limlst])
quad_qawf.summary: Calculates a Fourier cosine or Fourier sine transform on a semi-infinite interval using the Quadpack QAWF function.  The same approach as in quad_qawo is applied on successive finite intervals, and convergence acceleration by means of the Epsilon algorithm (Wynn, 1956) is applied to the series of the integral contributions.
signatures: quad_qawf(f(x), x, a, omega, trig, [epsabs, limit, maxp1, limlst])
quad_qawf(f, x, a, omega, trig, [epsabs, limit, maxp1, limlst])

quad_qawo.signature: quad_qawo(f(x), x, a, b, omega, trig, [epsrel, epsabs, limit, maxp1, limlst])
quad_qawo.summary: Integration of $\cos(\omega x) f(x)$ or $\sin(\omega x)$ over a finite interval, where $\omega$ is a constant. The rule evaluation component is based on the modified Clenshaw-Curtis technique.  quad_qawo applies adaptive subdivision with extrapolation, similar to quad_qags.
signatures: quad_qawo(f(x), x, a, b, omega, trig, [epsrel, epsabs, limit, maxp1, limlst])
quad_qawo(f, x, a, b, omega, trig, [epsrel, epsabs, limit, maxp1, limlst])

quad_qaws.signature: quad_qaws(f(x), x, a, b, alpha, beta, wfun, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qaws.summary: Integration of $w(x) f(x)$ over a finite interval, where $w(x)$ is a certain algebraic or logarithmic function.  A globally adaptive subdivision strategy is applied, with modified Clenshaw-Curtis integration on the subintervals which contain the endpoints of the interval of integration.
signatures: quad_qaws(f(x), x, a, b, alpha, beta, wfun, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])
quad_qaws(f, x, a, b, alpha, beta, wfun, [epsrel, epsabs, limit])

quantile.summary: This is the p-quantile, with p a number in $[0, 1]$, of the sample list. Although there are several definitions for the sample quantile (Hyndman, R. J., Fan, Y. (1996) Sample quantiles in statistical packages. American Statistician, 50, 361-365), the one based on linear interpolation is implemented in package Package-descriptive

quantile_bernoulli.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Bernoulli}(p)$ random variable, with $0 \leq p \leq 1$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_bernoulli. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_beta.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Beta}(a,b)$ random variable, with $a,b>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_beta. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_binomial.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Binomial}(n,p)$ random variable, with $0 \leq p \leq 1$ and $n$ a positive integer; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_binomial. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_cauchy.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Cauchy}(a,b)$ random variable, with $b>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_cauchy. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_chi2.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a Chi-square random variable $\chi^2(n)$ , with $n>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_chi2. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$.

quantile_continuous_uniform.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it ContinuousUniform}(a,b)$ random variable, with $a \lt b$ ; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_continuous_uniform. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_discrete_uniform.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it DiscreteUniform}(n)$ random variable, with $n$ a strictly positive integer; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_discrete_uniform. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_exp.summary: Returns the q-quantile of an ${\it Exponential}(m)$ random variable, with $m>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_exp. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$.

quantile_f.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a F random variable $F(m,n)$, with $m,n>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_f. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$.

quantile_gamma.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a $\Gamma\left(a,b\right)$ random variable, with $a,b>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_gamma. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_general_finite_discrete.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a general finite discrete random variable, with vector probabilities $v$.

quantile_geometric.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Geometric}(p)$ random variable, with $0 \lt p \le 1$ ; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_geometric. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$.

quantile_gumbel.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Gumbel}(a,b)$ random variable, with $b>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_gumbel. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_hypergeometric.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Hypergeometric}(n1,n2,n)$ random variable, with n1, n2 and n non negative integers and $n\leq n1+n2$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_hypergeometric. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_laplace.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Laplace}(a,b)$ random variable, with $b>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_laplace. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_logistic.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Logistic}(a,b)$ random variable , with $b>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_logistic. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_lognormal.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Lognormal}(m,s)$ random variable, with $s>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_lognormal. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_negative_binomial.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it NegativeBinomial}(n,p)$ random variable, with $0 < p \leq 1$ and $n$ a positive number; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_negative_binomial. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_noncentral_chi2.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a noncentral Chi-square random variable m4_noncentral_chi2(n,ncp) , with $n>0$ and noncentrality parameter $ncp \ge 0$ ; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_noncentral_chi2. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$.

quantile_noncentral_student_t.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a noncentral Student random variable ${\it nc\_t}(n, ncp)$ , with $n>0$ degrees of freedom and noncentrality parameter $ncp$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_noncentral_student_t. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_normal.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Normal}(m, s)$ random variable, with $s>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_005fnormal. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_pareto.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Pareto}(a,b)$ random variable, with $a,b>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_pareto. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_poisson.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Poisson}(m)$ random variable, with $m>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_poisson. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_rayleigh.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Rayleigh}(b)$ random variable, with $b>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_rayleigh. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$.

quantile_student_t.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a Student random variable $t(n)$ , with $n>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_student_t. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quantile_weibull.summary: Returns the q-quantile of a ${\it Weibull}(a,b)$ random variable, with $a,b>0$; in other words, this is the inverse of cdf_weibull. Argument q must be an element of $[0,1]$. To make use of this function, write first load("distrib").

quartile_skewness.summary: The quartile skewness coefficient, defined as

qubits.summary: qubits(n) returns a list representing the ground state of a system of n qubits.

quit.summary: Terminates the Maxima session.  Note that the function must be invoked as quit(); or quit()$, not quit by itself. quit supports returning an exit code to the shell for Lisps and OSes that support exit codes.  The default exit code is 0 (usually indicating no errors encountered).  Thus quit(1) indicates to the shell that maxima exited with some kind of failure.  This is useful in scripts where maxima can indicate to the shell that maxima failed to compute something or some other bad thing happened.

qunit.summary: Returns the principal unit of the real quadratic number field sqrt (n) where n is an integer, i.e., the element whose norm is unity. This amounts to solving Pell’s equation a^2 - n b^2 = 1.

quotient.summary: Returns the polynomial p_1 divided by the polynomial p_2.  The arguments x_1, ..., x_n are interpreted as in ratvars.

racah_v.signature: racah_v(a, b, c, a1, b1, c1)
racah_v.summary: Compute Racah’s V coefficient (computed in terms of a related Clebsch-Gordan coefficient).
signatures: racah_v(a, b, c, a1, b1, c1)

racah_w.signature: racah_w(j1, j2, j5, j4, j3, j6)
racah_w.summary: Compute Racah’s W coefficient (computed in terms of a Wigner 6j symbol)
signatures: racah_w(j1, j2, j5, j4, j3, j6)

radcan.summary: Simplifies expr, which can contain logs, exponentials, and radicals, by converting it into a form which is canonical over a large class of expressions and a given ordering of variables; that is, all functionally equivalent forms are mapped into a unique form.  For a somewhat larger class of expressions, radcan produces a regular form.  Two equivalent expressions in this class do not necessarily have the same appearance, but their difference can be simplified by radcan to zero.

random.summary: Returns a pseudorandom number.  If x is an integer, random (x) returns an integer from 0 through x - 1 inclusive.  If x is a floating point number, random (x) returns a nonnegative floating point number less than x.  random complains with an error if x is neither an integer nor a float, or if x is not positive.

random_bernoulli.summary: Returns a ${\it Bernoulli}(p)$ random variate, with $0 \leq p \leq 1$. Calling random_bernoulli with a second argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_beta.summary: Returns a ${\it Beta}(a,b)$ random variate, with $a,b>0$. Calling random_beta with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_binomial.summary: Returns a ${\it Binomial}(n,p)$ random variate, with $0 \leq p \leq 1$ and $n$ a positive integer. Calling random_binomial with a third argument m, a random sample of size m will be simulated.

random_bipartite_graph.summary: Returns a random bipartite graph on a+b vertices. Each edge is present with probability p.

random_cauchy.summary: Returns a ${\it Cauchy}(a,b)$ random variate, with $b>0$. Calling random_cauchy with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_chi2.summary: Returns a Chi-square random variate $\chi^2(n)$ , with $n>0$. Calling random_chi2 with a second argument m, a random sample of size m will be simulated.

random_continuous_uniform.summary: Returns a ${\it ContinuousUniform}(a,b)$ random variate, with $a \lt b.$ Calling random_continuous_uniform with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_digraph.summary: Returns a random directed graph on n vertices. Each arc is present with probability p.

random_discrete_uniform.summary: Returns a ${\it DiscreteUniform}(n)$ random variate, with $n$ a strictly positive integer. Calling random_discrete_uniform with a second argument m, a random sample of size m will be simulated.

random_exp.summary: Returns an ${\it Exponential}(m)$ random variate, with $m>0$. Calling random_exp with a second argument k, a random sample of size k will be simulated.

random_f.summary: Returns a F random variate $F(m,n)$, with $m,n>0$. Calling random_f with a third argument k, a random sample of size k will be simulated.

random_gamma.summary: Returns a $\Gamma\left(a,b\right)$ random variate, with $a,b>0$. Calling random_gamma with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_general_finite_discrete.summary: Returns a general finite discrete random variate, with vector probabilities $v$. Calling random_general_finite_discrete with a second argument m, a random sample of size m will be simulated.

random_geometric.summary: random_geometric(p) returns one random sample from a ${\it Geometric}(p)$ distribution, with $0 \lt p \le 1.$

random_graph.summary: Returns a random graph on n vertices. Each edge is present with probability p.

random_graph1.summary: Returns a random graph on n vertices and random m edges.

random_gumbel.summary: Returns a ${\it Gumbel}(a,b)$ random variate, with $b>0$. Calling random_gumbel with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_hypergeometric.summary: Returns a ${\it Hypergeometric}(n1,n2,n)$ random variate, with n1, n2 and n non negative integers and $n \le n_1 + n_2.$ Calling random_hypergeometric with a fourth argument m, a random sample of size m will be simulated.

random_laplace.summary: Returns a ${\it Laplace}(a,b)$ random variate, with $b>0$. Calling random_laplace with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_logistic.summary: Returns a ${\it Logistic}(a,b)$ random variate, with $b>0$. Calling random_logistic with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_lognormal.summary: Returns a ${\it Lognormal}(m,s)$ random variate, with $s>0$. Calling random_lognormal with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_negative_binomial.summary: Returns a ${\it NegativeBinomial}(n,p)$ random variate, with $0 < p \leq 1$ and $n$ a positive number. Calling random_negative_binomial with a third argument m, a random sample of size m will be simulated.

random_network.summary: Returns a random network on n vertices. Each arc is present with probability p and has a weight in the range [0,w]. The function returns a list [network, source, sink].

random_noncentral_chi2.summary: Returns a noncentral Chi-square random variate m4_noncentral_chi2(n,ncp) , with $n>0$ and noncentrality parameter $ncp \ge 0.$ Calling random_noncentral_chi2 with a third argument m, a random sample of size m will be simulated.

random_noncentral_student_t.summary: Returns a noncentral Student random variate ${\it nc\_t}(n, ncp)$ , with $n>0$. Calling random_noncentral_student_t with a third argument m, a random sample of size m will be simulated.

random_normal.summary: Returns a ${\it Normal}(m, s)$ random variate, with $s>0$. Calling random_normal with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_pareto.summary: Returns a ${\it Pareto}(a,b)$ random variate, with $a>0,b>0$. Calling random_pareto with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_perm.summary: Returns a random permutation of degree n.

random_permutation.summary: Returns a random permutation of the set or list a, as constructed by the Knuth shuffle algorithm.

random_poisson.summary: Returns a ${\it Poisson}(m)$ random variate, with $m>0$. Calling random_poisson with a second argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_rayleigh.summary: Returns a ${\it Rayleigh}(b)$ random variate, with $b>0$. Calling random_rayleigh with a second argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

random_regular_graph.summary: Returns a random d-regular graph on n vertices. The default value for d is d=3.

random_student_t.summary: Returns a Student random variate $t(n)$ , with $n>0$. Calling random_student_t with a second argument m, a random sample of size m will be simulated.

random_tournament.summary: Returns a random tournament on n vertices.

random_tree.summary: Returns a random tree on n vertices.

random_weibull.summary: Returns a ${\it Weibull}(a,b)$ random variate, with $a,b>0$. Calling random_weibull with a third argument n, a random sample of size n will be simulated.

range.summary: The range is the difference between the extreme values.

rank.summary: Computes the rank of the matrix M.  That is, the order of the largest non-singular subdeterminant of M.

ranks.summary: Returns the ranks of the elements of the list L as ordered by the predicate P, and handling elements which are equivalent under P by ties_method.

rassociative.summary: declare (g, rassociative) tells the Maxima simplifier that g is right-associative.  E.g., g(g(a, b), g(c, d)) simplifies to g(a, g(b, g(c, d))).

rat.summary: Converts expr to canonical rational expression (CRE) form by expanding and combining all terms over a common denominator and cancelling out the greatest common divisor of the numerator and denominator, as well as converting floating point numbers to rational numbers within a tolerance of ratepsilon. The variables are ordered according to the x_1, ..., x_n, if specified, as in ratvars.

ratchristof.summary: Causes rational simplification to be applied by christof.

ratcoef.summary: Returns the coefficient of the expression x^n in the expression expr. If omitted, n is assumed to be 1.

ratdenom.summary: Returns the denominator of expr, after coercing expr to a canonical rational expression (CRE). The return value is a CRE.

ratdiff.summary: Differentiates the rational expression expr with respect to x. expr must be a ratio of polynomials or a polynomial in x. The argument x may be a variable or a subexpression of expr.

ratdisrep.summary: Returns its argument as a general expression. If expr is a general expression, it is returned unchanged.

ratexpand.summary: Expands expr by multiplying out products of sums and exponentiated sums, combining fractions over a common denominator, cancelling the greatest common divisor of the numerator and denominator, then splitting the numerator (if a sum) into its respective terms divided by the denominator.

ratinterpol.summary: Generates a rational interpolator for data given by points and the degree of the numerator being equal to numdeg; the degree of the denominator is calculated automatically. Argument points must be either:

rational.summary: declare(a, rational) or declare(a, irrational) tells Maxima to recognize a as a rational or irrational real variable.

rationalize.summary: Convert all double floats and big floats in the Maxima expression expr to their exact rational equivalents.  If you are not familiar with the binary representation of floating point numbers, you might be surprised that rationalize (0.1) does not equal 1/10.  This behavior isn’t special to Maxima – the number 1/10 has a repeating, not a terminating, binary representation.

ratnumer.summary: Returns the numerator of expr, after coercing expr to a canonical rational expression (CRE). The return value is a CRE.

ratnump.summary: Returns true if expr is a literal integer or ratio of literal integers, otherwise false.

ratp.summary: Returns true if expr is a canonical rational expression (CRE) or extended CRE, otherwise false.

ratp_coeffs.summary: Returns the powers and coefficients of x in ratnumer(expr) as a list of length-2 lists; returned coefficients are in CRE form except for numbers.

ratp_dense_coeffs.summary: Returns the coefficients of powers of x in ratnumer(expr) from highest to lowest; returned coefficients are in CRE form except for numbers.

ratp_hipow.summary: Returns the highest power of x in ratnumer(expr)

ratp_lopow.summary: Returns the lowest power of x in ratnumer(expr)

ratsimp.summary: Simplifies the expression expr and all of its subexpressions, including the arguments to non-rational functions.  The result is returned as the quotient of two polynomials in a recursive form, that is, the coefficients of the main variable are polynomials in the other variables.  Variables may include non-rational functions (e.g., sin (x^2 + 1)) and the arguments to any such functions are also rationally simplified.

ratsubst.summary: Substitutes a for b in c and returns the resulting expression.

ratvars.summary: Declares main variables x_1, ..., x_n for rational expressions. x_n, if present in a rational expression, is considered the main variable. Otherwise, x_[n-1] is considered the main variable if present, and so on through the preceding variables to x_1, which is considered the main variable only if none of the succeeding variables are present.

ratweight.summary: Assigns a weight w_i to the variable x_i. This causes a term to be replaced by 0 if its weight exceeds the value of the variable ratwtlvl (default yields no truncation). The weight of a term is the sum of the products of the weight of a variable in the term times its power. For example, the weight of 3 x_1^2 x_2 is 2 w_1 + w_2. Truncation according to ratwtlvl is carried out only when multiplying or exponentiating canonical rational expressions (CRE).

read.summary: Prints expr_1, ..., expr_n, then reads one expression from the console and returns the evaluated expression.  The expression is terminated with a semicolon ; or dollar sign $.

read_array.summary: Reads the source S into the array A, until A is full or the source is exhausted. Input data are read into the array in row-major order; the input need not conform to the dimensions of A.

read_binary_array.summary: Reads binary 8-byte floating point numbers from the source S into the array A until A is full, or the source is exhausted. A must be an array created by array or make_array. Elements of A are read in row-major order.

read_binary_list.summary: read_binary_list(S) reads the entire content of the source S as a sequence of binary 8-byte floating point numbers, and returns it as a list. The source S may be a file name or a stream.

read_binary_matrix.summary: Reads binary 8-byte floating point numbers from the source S into the matrix M until M is full, or the source is exhausted. Elements of M are read in row-major order.

read_hashed_array.summary: Reads the source S and returns its entire content as a hashed-array. The source S may be a file name or a stream.

read_list.summary: read_list(S) reads the source S and returns its entire content as a flat list.

read_matrix.summary: read_matrix(S) reads the source S and returns its entire content as a matrix. The size of the matrix is inferred from the input data; each line of the file becomes one row of the matrix. If some lines have different lengths, read_matrix complains.

read_nested_list.summary: Reads the source S and returns its entire content as a nested list. The source S may be a file name or a stream.

read_xpm.summary: Reads a file in xpm and returns a picture object.

readbyte.summary: Removes and returns the first byte in stream which must be a binary input stream. If the end of file is encountered readbyte returns false.

readchar.summary: Removes and returns the first character in stream. If the end of file is encountered readchar returns false.

readline.summary: Returns a string containing all characters starting at the current position in stream up to the end of the line or false if the end of the file is encountered.

readonly.summary: Prints expr_1, ..., expr_n, then reads one expression from the console and returns the expression (without evaluation).  The expression is terminated with a ; (semicolon) or $ (dollar sign).

real.summary: declare(a, real), declare(a, imaginary), or declare(a, complex) tells Maxima to recognize a as a real, pure imaginary, or complex variable.

real_fft.summary: Computes the fast Fourier transform of a real-valued sequence x.  This is equivalent to performing fft(x), except that only the first N/2+1 results are returned, where N is the length of x.  N must be power of two.

real_imagpart_to_conjugate.summary: The function real_imagpart_to_conjugate replaces all occurrences of realpart and imagpart to algebraically equivalent expressions involving the conjugate.

realpart.summary: Returns the real part of expr.  realpart and imagpart will work on expressions involving trigonometric and hyperbolic functions, as well as square root, logarithm, and exponentiation.

realroots.summary: Computes rational approximations of the real roots of the polynomial expr or polynomial equation eqn of one variable, to within a tolerance of bound.  Coefficients of expr or eqn must be literal numbers; symbol constants such as %pi are rejected.

rearray.summary: Changes the dimensions of an array. The new array will be filled with the elements of the old one in row-major order.  If the old array was too small, the remaining elements are filled with false, 0.0 or 0, depending on the type of the array.  The type of the array cannot be changed.

rectform.summary: Returns an expression a + b %i equivalent to expr, such that a and b are purely real.

rectform_log_if_constant.summary: The function rectform_log_if_constant converts all terms of the form log(c) to  rectform(log(c)), where c is either a declared constant expression or explicitly declared constant

recttopolar.summary: Translates complex values of the form $a + b i$ to the form $r e^{i t}$, where $a$ is the real part and $b$ is the imaginary part.  $a$ and $b$ are 1-dimensional arrays of the same size. The array size need not be a power of 2.

rediff.summary: Evaluates all occurrences of the idiff command in the tensorial expression ten.

reduce_consts.summary: Replaces constant subexpressions of expr with constructed constant atoms, saving the definition of all these constructed constants in the list of equations const_eqns, and returning the modified expr.  Those parts of expr are constant which return true when operated on by the function constantp.  Hence, before invoking reduce_consts, one should do

reduce_order.summary: Reduces the order of linear recurrence rec when a particular solution sol is known. The reduced recurrence can be used to get other solutions.

regex_compile.summary: Compile regex string in pattern to an internal form that is easier for the regex engine to process.  This is not required, however.  All the regex functions accept this compiled regex or a string.  If the pattern is used many times, compiling the pattern will speed up matching.

regex_match.summary: regex_match is very similar to regex_match_pos except that it returns the matching substrings instead of the indices of the match.  If no match is found, returns false.

regex_match_pos.summary: Return a list consisting of a list of the start and end positions of str where the first match of regex occurred.  If no match is found, returns false.

regex_split.summary: Returns a list of strings where str has been split into substrings where the regex identifies the delimiters to use for separating the substrings.

regex_subst.summary: Returns a string where every occurrence of pattern has been replaced by replacement in the string str.

regex_subst_first.summary: Returns a string where the first occurrence of pattern in str with replacement.

region.signature: region(expr, var1, minval1, maxval1, var2, minval2, maxval2)
region.summary: Plots a region on the plane defined by inequalities.
signatures: region(expr, var1, minval1, maxval1, var2, minval2, maxval2)

region_boundaries.summary: Detects polygonal segments of global variable boundaries_array fully contained in the rectangle with vertices (x1,y1) -upper left- and (x2,y2) -bottom right-.

region_boundaries_plus.summary: Detects polygonal segments of global variable boundaries_array containing at least one vertex in the rectangle defined by vertices (x1,y1) -upper left- and (x2,y2) -bottom right-.

rem.summary: Removes the property indicated by indicator from atom. rem reverses the effect of put.

remainder.summary: Returns the remainder of the polynomial p_1 divided by the polynomial p_2.  The arguments x_1, ..., x_n are interpreted as in ratvars.

remarray.summary: Removes arrays and array associated functions and frees the storage occupied. The arguments may be declared arrays, hashed arrays, array functions, and subscripted functions.

remcomps.summary: Unbinds all values from tensor which were assigned with the components function.

remcon.summary: Removes all the contraction properties from the (tensor_1, ..., tensor_n). remcon(all) removes all contraction properties from all indexed objects.

remcoord.summary: Removes the coordinate differentiation property from the tensor_i that was established by the function coord.  remcoord(all) removes this property from all indexed objects.

remfun.summary: remfun (f, expr) replaces all occurrences of f (arg) by arg in expr.

remfunction.summary: Unbinds the function definitions of the symbols f_1, ..., f_n. The arguments may be the names of ordinary functions (created by := or define) or macro functions (created by ::=).

remlet.summary: Deletes the substitution rule, prod --> repl, most recently defined by the let function.  If name is supplied the rule is deleted from the rule package name.

remove_constvalue.summary: Reverts the effect of declare_005fconstvalue. This function should be loaded with load ("ezunits").

remove_dimensions.summary: Reverts the effect of declare_dimensions. This function should be loaded with load ("ezunits").

remove_edge.summary: Removes the edge e from the graph gr.

remove_index_properties.summary: Removes the properties established by declare_index_properties. All index properties are removed from each symbol a, b, c, ....

remove_plot_option.summary: Removes the global value of an option. The name of the option must be given.

remove_vertex.summary: Removes the vertex v from the graph gr.

rempart.summary: Removes part n from the expression expr.

remrule.summary: Removes rules defined by tellsimp or tellsimpafter.

remsym.summary: Removes all symmetry properties from tensor which has m covariant indices and n contravariant indices.

remvalue.summary: Removes the values of user variables name_1, ..., name_n (which can be subscripted) from the system.

rename.summary: Returns an expression equivalent to expr but with the dummy indices in each term chosen from the set [%1, %2,...], if the optional second argument is omitted. Otherwise, the dummy indices are indexed beginning at the value of count.  Each dummy index in a product will be different. For a sum, rename will operate upon each term in the sum resetting the counter with each term. In this way rename can serve as a tensorial simplifier. In addition, the indices will be sorted alphanumerically (if allsym is true) with respect to covariant or contravariant indices depending upon the value of flipflag. If flipflag is false then the indices will be renamed according to the order of the contravariant indices. If flipflag is true the renaming will occur according to the order of the covariant indices. It often happens that the combined effect of the two renamings will reduce an expression more than either one by itself.

reset.summary: Resets many global variables and options, and some other variables, to their default values.

reset_displays.summary: Resets the prompt prefix and suffix to the empty string, and sets both 1-d and 2-d display functions to the default.

reset_plot_options.summary: Sets the default global values of the plotting options. After changing the global values of some plotting options, this function is used to recovered the same values as when Maxima was started.

residue.summary: Computes the residue in the complex plane of the expression expr when the variable z assumes the value z_0.  The residue is the coefficient of (z - z_0)^(-1) in the Laurent series for expr.

resimplify.summary: Resimplifies the expression expr based on the current environment.  This function is useful when the fact database, option variables, or tellsimp rules have changed since the expression was last simplified.

resolvante.summary: calculates the resolvent of the polynomial P in x of degree n >= d by the function f expressed in the variables x_1, ..., x_d.  For efficiency of computation it is important to not include in the list [x_1, ..., x_d] variables which do not appear in the transformation function f.

resolvante_alternee1.summary: calculates the transformation P(x) of degree n by the function $product(x_i - x_j, 1 <= i < j <= n - 1)$.

resolvante_bipartite.summary: calculates the transformation of P(x) of even degree n by the function

resolvante_diedrale.summary: calculates the transformation of P(x) by the function x_1 x_2 + x_3 x_4.

resolvante_klein.summary: calculates the transformation of P(x) by the function x_1 x_2 x_4 + x_4.

resolvante_klein3.summary: calculates the transformation of P(x) by the function x_1 x_2 x_4 + x_4.

resolvante_produit_sym.summary: calculates the list of all product resolvents of the polynomial P(x).

resolvante_unitaire.summary: computes the resolvent of the polynomial P(x) by the polynomial Q(x).

resolvante_vierer.summary: computes the transformation of P(x) by the function x_1 x_2 - x_3 x_4.

rest.summary: Returns expr with its first n elements removed if n is positive and its last - n elements removed if n is negative. If n is 1 it may be omitted. The first argument expr may be a list, matrix, or other expression. When expr is an atom, rest signals an error; when expr is an empty list and partswitch is false, rest signals an error. When expr is an empty list and partswitch is true, rest returns end.

resultant.summary: The function resultant computes the resultant of the two polynomials p_1 and p_2, eliminating the variable x.  The resultant is a determinant of the coefficients of x in p_1 and p_2, which equals zero if and only if p_1 and p_2 have a non-constant factor in common.

return.summary: May be used to exit explicitly from the current block, while, for or do loop bringing its argument. It therefore can be compared with the return statement found in other programming languages but it yields one difference: In maxima only returns from the current block, not from the entire function it was called in. In this aspect it more closely resembles the break statement from C.

reveal.summary: Replaces parts of expr at the specified integer depth with descriptive summaries.

reverse.summary: Reverses the order of the members of the list (not the members themselves).  reverse also works on general expressions, e.g.  reverse(a=b); gives b=a.

revert.summary: These functions return the reversion of expr, a Taylor series about zero in the variable x.  revert returns a polynomial of degree equal to the highest power in expr.  revert2 returns a polynomial of degree n, which may be greater than, equal to, or less than the degree of expr.

rgb2level.summary: Transforms an rgb picture into a level one by averaging the red, green and blue channels.

rhs.summary: Returns the right-hand side (that is, the second argument) of the expression expr, when the operator of expr is one of the relational operators < <= = # equal notequal >= >,

ric.summary: The covariant Ricci-tensor. Computed by ricci.

ricci.summary: A function in the ctensor (component tensor) package. ricci computes the covariant (symmetric) components ric[i,j] of the Ricci tensor.  If the argument dis is true, then the non-zero components are displayed.

riem.summary: The (3,1) Riemann tensor. Computed when the function riemann is invoked. For information about index ordering, see the description of riemann.

riemann.summary: A function in the ctensor (component tensor) package.  riemann computes the Riemann curvature tensor from the given metric and the corresponding Christoffel symbols. The following index conventions are used:

rinvariant.summary: Forms the Kretschmann-invariant (kinvariant) obtained by contracting the tensors

risch.summary: Integrates expr with respect to x using the transcendental case of the Risch algorithm.  (The algebraic case of the Risch algorithm has not been implemented.)  This currently handles the cases of nested exponentials and logarithms which the main part of integrate can’t do.  integrate will automatically apply risch if given these cases.

rk.summary: The first form solves numerically one first-order ordinary differential equation, and the second form solves a system of m of those equations, using the 4th order Runge-Kutta method. var represents the dependent variable. ODE must be an expression that depends only on the independent and dependent variables and defines the derivative of the dependent variable with respect to the independent variable.

rncombine.summary: Transforms expr by combining all terms of expr that have identical denominators or denominators that differ from each other by numerical factors only.  This is slightly different from the behavior of combine, which collects terms that have identical denominators.

romberg.summary: Computes a numerical integration by Romberg’s method.

room.summary: Prints out a description of the state of storage and stack management in Maxima.  room calls the Lisp function of the same name.

rootscontract.summary: Converts products of roots into roots of products.  For example, rootscontract (sqrt(x)y^(3/2)) yields sqrt(xy^3).

round.summary: When x is a real number, returns the closest integer to x. Multiples of 1/2 are rounded to the nearest even integer.  Evaluation of x is similar to floor and ceiling.

row.summary: Returns the i’th row of the matrix M. The return value is a matrix.

rowop.summary: If M is a matrix, return the matrix that results from doing the row operation R_i <- R_i - theta  R_j.  If M doesn’t have a row i or j*, signal an error.

rowswap.summary: If M is a matrix, swap rows i and j.  If M doesn’t have a row i or j, signal an error.

rreduce.summary: Extends the binary function F to an n-ary function by composition, where s is a list.

rsetq.summary: Where var is any Maxima variable, matrix or array element, and exp is any Maxima expression which, after evaluation by Maxima results in an expression that can be translated by Gentran into the target language. This is equivalent to VAR : EVAL(EXP) ;

run_testsuite.summary: Run the Maxima test suite.  Tests producing the desired answer are considered “passes,” as are tests that do not produce the desired answer, but are marked as known bugs.

same_xy.summary: It can be either true or false. If true, the scales used in the x and y axes will be the same, in either 2d or 3d plots. See also yx_005fratio. same_xy and nosame_xy are synonyms for [same_xy, true] and [same_xy, false].

same_xyz.summary: It can be either true or false. If true, the scales used in the 3 axes of a 3d plot will be the same. same_xyz and nosame_xyz are synonyms for [same_xyz, true] and [same_xyz, false].

save.summary: Stores the current values of name_1, name_2, name_3, ..., in filename.  The arguments are the names of variables, functions, or other objects.  If a name has no value or function associated with it, it is ignored.  save returns filename.

saving.summary: The table that represents the values in a constant and periodic saving can be found by saving. amount represents the desired quantity and num the number of periods to save.

scalar.summary: declare(a, scalar) tells Maxima to consider a a scalar variable.

scalarp.summary: Returns true if expr is a number, constant, or variable declared scalar with declare, or composed entirely of numbers, constants, and such variables, but not containing matrices or lists.

scale_float.summary: scale_float scales the float f by the value 2^n.  This is done carefully so that no round-off every occurs.  If f is a float, then it is possible to underflow to 0 or overflow, depending on the value of f and n.  Bigfloats cannot underflow or overflow.

scaled_bessel_i.summary: The scaled modified Bessel function of the first kind of order $v$ and argument $z$.  That is,

scalefactors.summary: Here the argument coordinatetransform evaluates to the form [[expression1, expression2, ...], indeterminate1, indeterminat2, ...], where the variables indeterminate1, indeterminate2, etc. are the curvilinear coordinate variables and where a set of rectangular Cartesian components is given in terms of the curvilinear coordinates by [expression1, expression2, ...].  coordinates is set to the vector [indeterminate1, indeterminate2,...], and dimension is set to the length of this vector.  SF[1], SF[2], ..., SF[DIMENSION] are set to the coordinate scale factors, and sfprod is set to the product of these scale factors.  Initially, coordinates is [X, Y, Z], dimension is 3, and SF[1]=SF[2]=SF[3]=SFPROD=1, corresponding to 3-dimensional rectangular Cartesian coordinates.  To expand an expression into physical components in the current coordinate system, there is a function with usage of the form

scanmap.summary: Recursively applies f to expr, in a top down manner.  This is most useful when complete factorization is desired, for example:

scatterplot.summary: Plots scatter diagrams both for univariate (list) and multivariate (matrix) samples.

scatterplot_description.summary: Function scatterplot_description creates a graphic object suitable for creating complex scenes, together with other graphic objects.

scene.summary: Accepts an empty list or a list of several scene_005fobjects and scene_005foptions. The program launches Xmaxima, which opens an external window representing the given objects in a 3-dimensional space and applying the options given. Each object must belong to one of the following 4 classes: sphere, cube, cylinder or cone (see scene_005fobjects). Objects are identified by giving their name or by a list in which the first element is the class name and the following elements are options for that object.

schur2comp.summary: P is a polynomial in the variables of the list l_var.  Each of these variables represents a complete symmetric function.  In l_var the i-th complete symmetric function is represented by the concatenation of the letter h and the integer i: hi.  This function expresses P in terms of Schur functions.

sconcat.summary: Concatenates its arguments into a string.  Unlike concat, the arguments do not need to be atoms.

scopy.summary: Returns a copy of string as a new string.

scsimp.summary: Sequential Comparative Simplification (method due to Stoute). scsimp attempts to simplify expr according to the rules rule_1, ..., rule_n. If a smaller expression is obtained, the process repeats.  Otherwise after all simplifications are tried, it returns the original answer.

scurvature.summary: Returns the scalar curvature (obtained by contracting the Ricci tensor) of the Riemannian manifold with the given metric.

sdowncase.summary: Like supcase but uppercase characters are converted to lowercase.

second.summary: Returns the 2nd item of expression or list expr. See first for more details.

sequal.summary: Returns true if string_1 and string_2 contain the same sequence of characters.

sequalignore.summary: Like sequal but ignores case which is only possible for non-US-ASCII characters when the underlying Lisp is able to recognize a character as an alphabetic character. See remarks on alphacharp.

set_alt_display.summary: The input num is the display to set; it may be either 1 or 2. The second input display-function is the display function to use. The display function may be either a Maxima function or a lambda expression.

set_draw_defaults.summary: Sets user graphics options. This function is useful for plotting a sequence of graphics with common graphics options. Calling this function without arguments removes user defaults.

set_edge_weight.summary: Assigns the weight w to the edge e in the graph gr.

set_partitions.summary: Returns the set of all partitions of a, or a subset of that set.

set_plot_option.summary: Accepts any of the options listed in the section Plotting Options, and saves them for use in plotting commands. The values of the options set in each plotting command will have precedence, but if those options are not given, the default values set with this function will be used.

set_prompt.summary: Set the prompt prefix or suffix to expr. The input fix must evaluate to one of prefix, suffix, general, prolog or epilog. The input expr must evaluate to either a string or false; if false, the fix is reset to the default value.

set_random_state.summary: Copies s to the random number generator state.

set_up_dot_simplifications.summary: The eqns are polynomial equations in non commutative variables. The value of current_variables is the list of variables used for computing degrees.  The equations must be homogeneous, in order for the procedure to terminate.

set_vertex_label.summary: Assigns the label l to the vertex v in the graph gr.

setdifference.summary: Returns a set containing the elements in the set a that are not in the set b.

setelmx.summary: Assigns x to the (i, j)’th element of the matrix M, and returns the altered matrix.

setequalp.summary: Returns true if sets a and b have the same number of elements
